channel.title: Milvus Search on 小巷 Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Milvus Search on 小巷 Blog

item.title: Comparison and Practice of Four Milvus Text Retrieval Methods
item.description:
Coverage: LIKE, TEXT_MATCH, PHRASE_MATCH, full-text search (BM25) four text matching methods Official Documentation: Text Match | Full Text Search

I. Text Retrieval Overview

1.1 Text Retrieval vs Vector Search

Comparison Item Text Retrieval Vector Search
Matching Method Character/word matching based on text content Similarity calculation based on vectors
Query Input Text string or keywords Vector (embedding)
Involves Vector Calculation No (except full-text search optional) Yes
Result Sorting By primary key or relevance score By similarity from high to low
Typical Scenarios Keyword search, precise matching Semantic search, recommendation systems

1.2 Why Do We Need Full-text Search? Isn’t Vector Search Enough?

Plain Explanation: Dense Vectors vs Sparse Vectors

Your understanding is correct: Full-text search (BM25) indeed uses sparse vectors. Let’s illustrate the differences with examples:

Dense Vector Search 

Usage: Vectors generated by models like BERT, OpenAI embeddings
Features: Every dimension has values, vectors are "dense"
Example: Search "apple phone"
✅ Can match: "iPhone 13", "iOS device" (understands semantics)
❌ But may match: "Python 3.11 tutorial" (if searching "Python 3.11")
Because it understands "programming language" semantics, but insensitive to version numbers

Advantages: Understands semantics, “apple phone” = “iPhone” Disadvantages: Insensitive to precise keywords (like version numbers, models)

Sparse Vector Search (BM25) 

Usage: BM25 algorithm based on word frequency statistics
Features: Only dimensions with appearing words have values, vectors are "sparse"
Example: Search "Python 3.11 new features"
✅ Precise matching of documents containing "3.11"
✅ Precise matching of keywords "Python", "3.11", "new features"
❌ But doesn't understand "iPhone" and "apple phone" mean the same thing

Advantages: Precise keyword matching, sensitive to version numbers, models, proper nouns Disadvantages: Doesn’t understand semantics, “apple phone” ≠ “iPhone”

Practical Application Scenario Comparison

Scenario Recommended Method Reason
Search “machine learning beginner tutorial” Dense vector Need to understand “beginner”, “tutorial” semantics
Search “Python 3.11.5 bug” Sparse vector (BM25) Version number must match precisely
Search “iPhone 14 Pro Max” Sparse vector (BM25) Model must match precisely
Search “how to improve code quality” Dense vector Need to understand intent, match related content
Search “ISBN: 978-7-115-12345-6” Sparse vector (BM25) Precise matching of numbers

Why Does Milvus Support Both?

Best Practice: Hybrid Search

# Use both dense and sparse vectors simultaneously
# Dense vector: Understand semantics
# Sparse vector: Precise keyword matching
# Result fusion: Both semantic understanding and keyword precision

Typical RAG Application Scenario:

  • User asks: “What are the new features in Python 3.11?”
  • Dense vector: Find semantically related “Python new version introduction” documents
  • Sparse vector: Precisely match paragraphs containing “3.11”
  • Fused results: Both relevant and precise

Summary:

  • Full-text search (BM25) doesn’t replace vector search, but complements it
  • Dense vectors excel at semantic understanding, sparse vectors excel at precise matching
  • Often used together in practical applications to leverage respective advantages

1.3 Comparison of Four Text Retrieval Methods

Method LIKE TEXT_MATCH PHRASE_MATCH Full-text Search (BM25)
Core Features Character pattern matching Keyword precise matching Order-sensitive phrase matching Relevance search
Matching Logic Character-level matching OR logic (default) Strict order BM25 scoring sort
Tokenization No tokenization Smart tokenization Smart tokenization Smart tokenization
Scoring Mechanism No scoring No scoring No scoring BM25 scoring
Configuration Complexity Low Medium Medium High
Performance Fastest Fast Medium Medium
Field Requirements No special requirements Needs enable_match=True Needs enable_match=True Special configuration required

II. LIKE Operator: Character Pattern Matching

2.1 Core Features

  • Simplest text matching method: Character-level pattern matching
  • No tokenization processing: Direct string matching
  • Best performance: No complex text analysis involved
  • No special configuration required: Any VARCHAR field supports

2.2 Wildcard Explanation

Wildcard Meaning Example Matching Results
% Matches any length characters (including 0) "Python%" Python, Python programming, Python3
% Matches any length characters "%programming" Python programming, Java programming
% Matches any length characters "%machine learning%" Deep learning and machine learning, machine learning practice

2.3 Code Example 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient

client = MilvusClient(uri="http://localhost:19530")

# 1. Prefix matching
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter='title LIKE "Python%"',
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# 2. Suffix matching
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter='title LIKE "%programming"',
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# 3. Infix matching (contains)
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter='title LIKE "%machine learning%"',
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

2.4 Application Scenarios

  • Simple prefix, suffix, infix matching
  • Precise string lookup without tokenization
  • High performance requirement scenarios
  • English words or fixed format matching

2.5 Limitations

  • No smart tokenization support
  • Cannot handle synonyms
  • Unfriendly to Chinese tokenization
  • No relevance scoring

III. TEXT_MATCH: Keyword Matching

3.1 Core Features

  • Smart tokenization: Automatically tokenize query text
  • OR logic: Default uses OR logic to match multiple keywords
  • Supports logical combinations: Can combine with AND, OR, NOT
  • Field configuration required: Field must enable enable_match=True

3.2 Basic Usage 

# Single keyword matching
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python')",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# Multi-keyword matching (OR logic)
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python machine learning')",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)
# Matches records containing "Python" OR "machine learning"

3.3 Logical Combinations 

# AND logic: Contain multiple keywords simultaneously
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND TEXT_MATCH(title, 'programming')",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# NOT logic: Exclude specific keywords
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND NOT TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Web')",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# Combine with scalar filtering
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND rating >= 4.5",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"],
 limit=5
)

3.4 Application Scenarios

  • Keyword retrieval
  • Complex queries requiring logical combinations
  • Chinese tokenization scenarios
  • Queries combining with scalar filtering

IV. PHRASE_MATCH: Phrase Matching

4.1 Core Features

  • Order-sensitive: Strictly match word order
  • Precise phrase matching: Suitable for finding specific phrases
  • Supports slop parameter: Allows flexibility between words
  • Field configuration required: Field must enable enable_match=True

4.2 slop Parameter Details

slop Value Meaning Sample Query Matching Results
0 (default) Exact match, word order and position completely consistent “machine learning” ✓ “machine learning”✗ “machine boosts learning”
1 Allow one word gap or position adjustment “machine learning” ✓ “machine learning”✓ “machine boosts learning”
2 Allow two word gaps or word order reversal “machine learning” ✓ “learning machine”✓ “machine quickly boosts learning”

4.3 Code Example 

# Exact phrase matching (slop=0, default)
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="PHRASE_MATCH(title, 'Python programming')",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# Fuzzy matching with slop parameter
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="PHRASE_MATCH(title, 'machine learning', 2)",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=5
)

# Combine with scalar filtering
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="PHRASE_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND is_available == True",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available"],
 limit=5
)

4.4 Application Scenarios

  • Scenarios requiring precise phrase matching
  • Queries where word order matters (proper nouns, fixed collocations)
  • Fuzzy matching controlling word spacing
  • Finding quotations or specific expressions

V. Full-text Search (BM25)

5.1 Core Features

  • BM25 Algorithm: Probability retrieval model-based relevance scoring
  • Automatic vectorization: Text automatically converted to sparse vectors, no manual generation needed
  • Relevance sorting: Results returned sorted by BM25 scores
  • Suitable for RAG scenarios: Particularly suitable for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) applications

5.2 BM25 Algorithm Introduction

BM25 (Best Matching 25) is a ranking function for information retrieval, considering these factors:

  • Term Frequency (TF): Frequency of keywords appearing in documents
  • Inverse Document Frequency (IDF): Rarity of keywords across the entire collection
  • Document Length Normalization: Avoid bias toward longer documents

5.3 What Do You Need to Do? What Does Milvus Do?

Important Note: Sparse vectors are automatically generated!

Many people mistakenly think they need to manually generate both dense and sparse vectors, but actually:

Data Preparation Division of Labor

Data Type Do You Need to Generate Explanation
Raw Text ✅ Yes Store file content, for returning results and generating sparse vectors
Dense Vector ✅ Yes Generated using OpenAI/BERT models, for semantic search
Sparse Vector (BM25) ❌ No Automatically generated by Milvus (configure BM25 function)

Actual Workflow Example 

# ========== Step 1: Read file content ==========
file_content = "Python 3.11 introduces new exception group functionality, enhancing error handling capabilities..."

# ========== Step 2: Generate dense vector (you need to do) ==========
import openai

dense_vector = openai.Embedding.create(
 input=file_content,
 model="text-embedding-ada-002"
)['data'][0]['embedding']

# ========== Step 3: Insert into Milvus ==========
# Only need to provide: raw text + dense vector
# Sparse vector will be automatically generated!
client.insert('my_collection', [{
 "text": file_content, # Raw text
 "dense_vector": dense_vector, # Your generated dense vector
 # sparse_vector not needed, automatically generated by Milvus!
}])

# ========== Step 4: Hybrid search ==========
query = "Python 3.11 new features"

# Generate query dense vector (you need to do)
query_dense = openai.Embedding.create(
 input=query,
 model="text-embedding-ada-002"
)['data'][0]['embedding']

# Dense vector search request
dense_req = AnnSearchRequest(
 data=[query_dense],
 anns_field="dense_vector",
 param={"metric_type": "COSINE"},
 limit=5
)

# Sparse vector search request
# Note: Pass text directly, Milvus automatically converts to sparse vector!
sparse_req = AnnSearchRequest(
 data=[query], # Pass query text directly
 anns_field="sparse_vector",
 param={"metric_type": "BM25"},
 limit=5
)

# Execute hybrid search
result = client.hybrid_search(
 collection_name='my_collection',
 reqs=[dense_req, sparse_req],
 ranker=rrf_ranker,
 limit=10
)

Key Points Summary

✅ You only need to care about dense vector generation (using OpenAI, BERT, sentence-transformers, etc.)

✅ Sparse vectors completely handled by Milvus internally (automatically generated after configuring BM25 function)

✅ Don’t need to manually generate sparse vectors during search (pass text directly, Milvus automatically converts)

❌ Don’t need to implement BM25 algorithm yourself

❌ Don’t need to manually calculate term frequency and IDF

This is why Milvus’s full-text search is powerful: It encapsulates complex BM25 calculations, you only need to provide raw text!

5.4 Complete Configuration Process

Step 1: Create Schema Supporting Full-text Search

Need to define three required fields: primary key, text field, sparse vector field

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType, Function, FunctionType

client = MilvusClient(
 uri="http://localhost:19530",
 token="root:Milvus"
)

# Create Schema
schema = client.create_schema()

# 1. Primary key field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="id",
 datatype=DataType.INT64,
 is_primary=True,
 auto_id=True
)

# 2. Text field (enable tokenizer)
schema.add_field(
 field_name="text",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=1000,
 enable_analyzer=True # Must enable tokenizer
)

# 3. Sparse vector field (store BM25 vectors)
schema.add_field(
 field_name="sparse",
 datatype=DataType.SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR
)

Step 2: Define BM25 Function

BM25 function automatically converts text to sparse vectors:

# Define BM25 function
bm25_function = Function(
 name="text_bm25_emb", # Function name
 input_field_names=["text"], # Input: text field
 output_field_names=["sparse"], # Output: sparse vector field
 function_type=FunctionType.BM25, # Function type: BM25
)

# Add function to Schema
schema.add_function(bm25_function)

Step 3: Configure Sparse Vector Index 

# Prepare index parameters
index_params = client.prepare_index_params()

# Add index for sparse vector field
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="sparse",
 index_type="SPARSE_INVERTED_INDEX", # Sparse inverted index
 metric_type="BM25", # Metric type: BM25
 params={
 "inverted_index_algo": "DAAT_MAXSCORE", # Inverted index algorithm
 "bm25_k1": 1.2, # BM25 parameter k1 (term frequency saturation)
 "bm25_b": 0.75 # BM25 parameter b (length normalization)
 }
)

BM25 Parameter Explanation:

  • bm25_k1 (default 1.2): Controls term frequency saturation, higher values mean greater TF impact
  • bm25_b (default 0.75): Controls document length normalization, 0 means no normalization, 1 means full normalization

Step 4: Create Collection 

# Create collection
client.create_collection(
 collection_name='my_collection',
 schema=schema,
 index_params=index_params
)

Step 5: Insert Text Data

Insert raw text directly, BM25 function automatically generates sparse vectors:

# Insert text data (no need to manually generate vectors)
client.insert('my_collection', [
 {'text': 'information retrieval is a field of study.'},
 {'text': 'information retrieval focuses on finding relevant information in large datasets.'},
 {'text': 'data mining and information retrieval overlap in research.'},
])

Step 6: Execute Full-text Search 

# Execute full-text search
search_params = {
 "metric_type": "BM25",
 "params": {}
}

results = client.search(
 collection_name="my_collection",
 data=["What is information retrieval?"], # Query text
 anns_field="sparse", # Search sparse vector field
 search_params=search_params,
 limit=5,
 output_fields=["text"]
)

# Output results
for i, hit in enumerate(results[0]):
 print(f"{i+1}. Text: {hit['entity']['text']}")
 print(f" Relevance Score: {hit['distance']:.4f}\n")

5.5 Complete Example 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType, Function, FunctionType

# 1. Connect to Milvus
client = MilvusClient(uri="http://localhost:19530")

# 2. Create Schema
schema = client.create_schema()
schema.add_field(field_name="id", datatype=DataType.INT64, is_primary=True, auto_id=True)
schema.add_field(field_name="text", datatype=DataType.VARCHAR, max_length=1000, enable_analyzer=True)
schema.add_field(field_name="sparse", datatype=DataType.SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR)

# 3. Add BM25 function
bm25_function = Function(
 name="text_bm25_emb",
 input_field_names=["text"],
 output_field_names=["sparse"],
 function_type=FunctionType.BM25,
)
schema.add_function(bm25_function)

# 4. Configure index
index_params = client.prepare_index_params()
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="sparse",
 index_type="SPARSE_INVERTED_INDEX",
 metric_type="BM25",
 params={"bm25_k1": 1.2, "bm25_b": 0.75}
)

# 5. Create collection
client.create_collection(
 collection_name='full_text_demo',
 schema=schema,
 index_params=index_params
)

# 6. Insert data
client.insert('full_text_demo', [
 {'text': 'Artificial intelligence is transforming the world.'},
 {'text': 'Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence.'},
 {'text': 'Deep learning uses neural networks for complex tasks.'},
])

# 7. Execute search
results = client.search(
 collection_name="full_text_demo",
 data=["What is artificial intelligence?"],
 anns_field="sparse",
 search_params={"metric_type": "BM25"},
 limit=3,
 output_fields=["text"]
)

# 8. Output results
for i, hit in enumerate(results[0]):
 print(f"{i+1}. {hit['entity']['text']} (Score: {hit['distance']:.4f})")

5.6 Application Scenarios

  • Complex text content search
  • Scenarios requiring relevance sorting
  • Large-scale document retrieval
  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) applications
  • Search engine-like applications

5.7 Differences from TEXT_MATCH

Comparison Item TEXT_MATCH Full-text Search (BM25)
Return Results No scoring sort Sorted by BM25 scores
Vectorization Not involved Automatically generates sparse vectors
Configuration Complexity Low High
Relevance Scoring None Yes (BM25)
Application Scenarios Simple keyword matching Complex text retrieval, RAG

VI. Method Selection Guide

6.1 Decision Tree 

Need text retrieval?
├── Simple character matching?
│ └── Yes → Use LIKE (best performance)
└── No → Need tokenization
├── Precise phrase matching?
│ └── Yes → Use PHRASE_MATCH
├── Keyword retrieval?
│ └── Yes → Use TEXT_MATCH
└── Need relevance sorting?
└── Yes → Use full-text search (BM25)

6.2 Scenario Comparison Table

Scenario Recommended Method Reason
Search book titles starting with “Python” LIKE Simple prefix matching, best performance
Search book titles containing “machine learning” TEXT_MATCH Supports Chinese tokenization
Search phrase “deep learning practice” PHRASE_MATCH Order-sensitive
Search articles most relevant to “AI technology” Full-text search Need relevance scoring
Search books containing “Python” but not “Web” TEXT_MATCH Supports logical combinations

VII. Practice Recommendations

7.1 Performance Optimization 

# 1. Prioritize LIKE (best performance)
# Suitable: Simple prefix/suffix matching
filter='title LIKE "Python%"'

# 2. TEXT_MATCH suitable for most scenarios
# Suitable: Keyword retrieval, supports logical combinations
filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND rating >= 4.0"

# 3. PHRASE_MATCH for precise matching
# Suitable: Scenarios requiring word order preservation
filter="PHRASE_MATCH(title, 'Python programming')"

# 4. Full-text search for complex scenarios
# Suitable: Large-scale text search requiring relevance sorting

7.2 Field Configuration

To use TEXT_MATCH or PHRASE_MATCH, need to configure fields:

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType

schema = client.create_schema()

# Add field supporting text matching
schema.add_field(
 field_name="title",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=500,
 enable_match=True, # Enable text matching
 enable_analyzer=True # Enable tokenizer (optional)
)

7.3 Common Combination Patterns 

# Pattern 1: Text matching + scalar filtering
filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND rating >= 4.5 AND is_available == True"

# Pattern 2: Multi-field text matching
filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') OR TEXT_MATCH(description, 'Python')"

# Pattern 3: Phrase matching + exclusion
filter="PHRASE_MATCH(title, 'machine learning') AND NOT TEXT_MATCH(title, 'beginner')"

VIII. Common Issues

8.1 TEXT_MATCH Error

Error Message: field 'title' doesn't support text match

Cause: Field didn’t enable enable_match=True

Solution:

# Configure field when creating collection
schema.add_field(
 field_name="title",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=500,
 enable_analyzer=True, # Enable tokenizer
 enable_match=True # Must enable
)

8.2 LIKE Doesn’t Match Chinese

Cause: LIKE is character-level matching, Chinese tokenization may cause matching failure

Solutions:

# Solution 1: Use TEXT_MATCH (recommended)
filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'machine learning')"

# Solution 2: Use complete string
filter='title LIKE "%machine learning%"'

# Solution 3: Configure tokenizer method
analyzer_params = {
 "type": "english"
}
schema.add_field(
 field_name="title",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=500,
 enable_analyzer=True, # Enable tokenizer
 analyzer_params = analyzer_params,
 enable_match=True # Must enable
)

8.3 PHRASE_MATCH Doesn’t Match Results

Cause: Strict word order, may fail due to tokenization or word order issues

Solutions:

# Increase slop parameter, allow word gaps
filter="PHRASE_MATCH(title, 'machine learning', 2)"

# Or switch to TEXT_MATCH
filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'machine learning')"

IX. Chapter Knowledge Summary

Method Key Parameters Application Scenarios
LIKE % wildcards Simple character pattern matching
TEXT_MATCH Keyword strings Keyword retrieval, logical combinations
PHRASE_MATCH Phrase strings, slop Precise phrase matching
Full-text Search BM25 configuration Complex text search, relevance sorting

Key Points:

  • LIKE simplest, best performance, but limited functionality
  • TEXT_MATCH supports tokenization and logical combinations, suitable for most scenarios
  • PHRASE_MATCH suitable for precise matching requiring word order preservation
  • Full-text search most powerful, but complex configuration

X. Relationship with Other Query Methods

Text retrieval methods can be combined with previously learned query methods:

# Combine with Query
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python') AND rating >= 4.5",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"],
 limit=10
)

# Combine with Search (hybrid search)
result = client.search(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 filter="TEXT_MATCH(title, 'Python')", # Text filtering
 limit=5
)

item.title: Comprehensive Analysis of Milvus Scalar Query Methods
item.description:
Coverage: Scalar queries (Get, Query, QueryIterator), filtering expressions, filtering templates Official Documentation: Get & Scalar Query

I. Comprehensive Comparison of Milvus Five Query Methods

Milvus provides 5 query methods, divided into two major categories: scalar queries (3 methods) and vector searches (2 methods).

1.1 Complete Comparison Table of Five Methods

Comparison Item Get Query QueryIterator Search SearchIterator
Query Type Scalar Query Scalar Query Scalar Query Vector Search Vector Search
Query Basis Primary Key Filter Expression Filter Expression Vector Similarity Vector Similarity
Involves Vector Index No No No Yes Yes
Calculates Similarity No No No Yes Yes
Result Sorting By Primary Key Order By Primary Key or Insertion Order By Primary Key or Insertion Order By Similarity (High to Low) By Similarity (High to Low)
Return Quantity Control Primary Key Count limit Parameter batch_size Batching limit Parameter batch_size Batching
Data Volume Limit No Limit Default Maximum 16384 Records No Limit (Batching) Default Maximum 16384 Records No Limit (Batching)
Memory Usage Low Medium Low (Streaming) Medium Low (Streaming)
Query Speed Fastest Fast Medium (Batching) Medium (ANN Calculation) Slow (Batching + ANN)
Typical Scenarios Known Primary Key Precise Lookup Conditional Filtering Large Data Export Semantic Search, Recommendation Large-scale Vector Retrieval
Supports Filter Conditions No Yes Yes Yes (Combined with Vector Search) Yes (Combined with Vector Search)
Required Parameters ids filter filter, batch_size data, anns_field data, anns_field, batch_size

1.2 Scalar Query vs Vector Search

Scalar Queries (Get / Query / QueryIterator):

  • No vector calculations involved, fast query speed
  • Based on primary key index or scalar field index
  • Suitable for precise lookups, conditional filtering, data export
  • Results have no similarity sorting

Vector Searches (Search / SearchIterator):

  • Involves vector similarity calculation (ANN search)
  • Based on vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, etc.)
  • Suitable for semantic search, recommendation systems, similar content lookup
  • Results sorted by similarity, includes distance field

1.3 Method Selection Decision Tree 

Need to query data?
├── Based on vector similarity?
│ ├── Yes → Vector Search
│ │ ├── Result count < 16384?
│ │ │ └── Yes → Use Search
│ │ └── Result count > 16384?
│ │ └── Yes → Use SearchIterator
│ └── No → Scalar Query
│ ├── Known primary key?
│ │ └── Yes → Use Get (Fastest)
│ └── No → Conditional filtering, judge data volume
│ ├── Result count < 10,000 → Use Query
│ └── Result count > 10,000 → Use QueryIterator

Scalar queries refer to query methods that do not involve vector similarity calculations, only performing data retrieval through primary keys or scalar field conditions. This section focuses on the three scalar query methods: Get, Query, and QueryIterator.

II. Get Method: Query by Primary Key

2.1 Core Features

  • Fastest query method: Direct lookup through primary key index, time complexity O(1)
  • No vector calculations involved: Pure scalar field query
  • Precise matching: Must provide accurate primary key values

2.2 Code Example 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient

client = MilvusClient(uri="http://localhost:19530")

# Get entities by primary key
result = client.get(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 ids=[1, 5, 10], # Primary key list
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "isbn", "rating"]
)

# Iterate through results
for record in result:
 print(f"ID: {record['book_id']}, Title: {record['title']}")

2.3 Parameter Explanation

Parameter Type Required Description
collection_name str Yes Collection name
ids List Yes Primary key list
output_fields List[str] No List of fields to return, defaults to primary key only
partition_names List[str] No Limit query to specific partitions

2.4 Return Result Structure 

result = [
 {
 'book_id': 1,
 'title': 'Python Programming',
 'isbn': '978-1234567890',
 'rating': 4.5
 },
 {
 'book_id': 5,
 'title': 'AI Practice',
 'isbn': '978-0987654321',
 'rating': 4.8
 }
]

III. Query Method: Conditional Filtering Query

3.1 Core Features

  • Flexible conditional filtering: Supports rich filter expressions
  • Controllable return quantity: Limit result count via limit parameter
  • No vector calculations involved: Pure scalar field query

3.2 Basic Usage 

# Simple condition query
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="is_available == True",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available"],
 limit=10
)

# Composite condition query
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="is_available == True and rating >= 4.0",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"],
 limit=5
)

3.3 Parameter Explanation

Parameter Type Required Description
collection_name str Yes Collection name
filter str Yes Filter expression (empty string means query all)
output_fields List[str] No List of fields to return
limit int No Maximum number of results, default 16384
partition_names List[str] No Limit query to specific partitions
filter_params dict No Filter template parameters (see Section 3.6)

IV. Detailed Filtering Expressions

4.1 Comparison Operators 

# Equal
filter = "is_available == True"
filter = "rating == 4.5"

# Not equal
filter = "rating != 3.0"

# Greater than/Less than
filter = "rating > 4.0"
filter = "price < 100.0"
filter = "page_count >= 200"
filter = "rating <= 5.0"

4.2 Range Operators 

# IN operator
filter = "rating in [4.0, 4.5, 5.0]"
filter = "book_id in [1, 2, 3, 5, 8]"

# LIKE operator (fuzzy matching)
filter = 'title like "%Python%"' # Contains "Python"
filter = 'title like "Python%"' # Starts with "Python"
filter = 'title like "%Programming"' # Ends with "Programming"

4.3 Logical Operators 

# AND
filter = "is_available == True and rating >= 4.0"

# OR
filter = 'title like "%Python%" or title like "%AI%"'

# NOT
filter = "not is_available == True"

# Complex combination
filter = """
(is_available == True and rating >= 4.0) and
(price < 150.0 or page_count > 300)
"""

4.4 Null Value Checking 

# Not null
filter = "details is not null"

# Null value
filter = "description is null"

4.5 Arithmetic Operators 

# Modulo (find even ID)
filter = "book_id % 2 == 0"

# Multiplication
filter = "price * 0.8 < 100"

# Division
filter = "page_count / 100 > 2"

# Addition/Subtraction
filter = "rating + 0.5 >= 5.0"

4.6 Filtering Templates

Why do we need filtering templates?

  • Improve performance when processing CJK (Chinese/Japanese/Korean) characters
  • Enhance code readability and security
  • Query engine can cache parsed expression structures

Basic Usage:

# Define template (using {param_name} placeholder)
template_filter = "rating >= {min_rating} and is_available == {available}"

# Pass parameters
template_params = {
 "min_rating": 4.0,
 "available": True
}

# Execute query
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter=template_filter,
 filter_params=template_params,
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"],
 limit=10
)

Array IN Operation Template:

# Template definition
in_filter = "rating in {ratings}"

# Parameter passing
in_params = {"ratings": [4.0, 4.5, 5.0]}

result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter=in_filter,
 filter_params=in_params,
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"],
 limit=10
)

Notes:

  • Placeholder names must exactly match keys in filter_params
  • Ensure parameter values match field data types
  • Requires Milvus 2.4+ version support

V. QueryIterator Method: Pagination Query

5.1 Core Features

  • Memory-friendly: Stream processing, doesn’t load all results at once
  • Suitable for large data volumes: Batch retrieval avoids memory overflow
  • No vector calculations involved: Pure scalar field query

5.2 Code Example 

# Create query iterator
iterator = client.query_iterator(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="is_available == True",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available"],
 batch_size=50 # Return 50 records per batch
)

# Retrieve results in batches
all_results = []
while True:
 batch_result = iterator.next()
 if not batch_result:
 break

 all_results.extend(batch_result)
 print(f"This batch returned {len(batch_result)} records")

print(f"Total retrieved {len(all_results)} records")

5.3 Parameter Explanation

Parameter Type Required Description
collection_name str Yes Collection name
filter str Yes Filter expression
output_fields List[str] No List of fields to return
batch_size int Yes Number of records per batch
partition_names List[str] No Limit query to specific partitions

5.4 Usage Scenarios

  • Need to export large amounts of data
  • Need to traverse all matching records in collection
  • Memory-constrained environments
  • Need real-time processing of query results (streaming processing)

VI. Special Query Techniques

6.1 Count Entity Numbers 

# Count total in collection
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="",
 output_fields=["count(*)"]
)
print(result) # [{'count(*)': 2000}]

# Count satisfying conditions
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="rating >= 4.5",
 output_fields=["count(*)"]
)

6.2 Query All Data 

# Empty filter expression queries all
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"],
 limit=100
)

6.3 Partition Query 

# Limit query to specific partition
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="rating >= 4.0",
 partition_names=["partition_2024"],
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"]
)

VII. Method Selection Guide

7.1 Decision Tree 

Need to query data?
├── Known primary key?
│ └── Yes → Use Get (Fastest)
└── No → Need conditional filtering
├── Small data volume (< 10,000 records)?
│ └── Yes → Use Query
└── Large data volume (> 10,000 records)?
└── Yes → Use QueryIterator

7.2 Performance Comparison

Method Query Speed Memory Usage Suitable Data Volume
Get Fastest Low Any
Query Fast Medium < 10,000 records
QueryIterator Medium Low (Streaming) Any

VIII. Common Issues

8.1 Query Returns Empty Results

Possible Causes:

  • Filter conditions too strict
  • Collection not loaded into memory
  • Field name spelling errors

Solutions:

# 1. Check collection load status
load_state = client.get_load_state(collection_name="book_collection")
print(load_state)

# 2. Simplify filter conditions for testing
result = client.query(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="", # Empty filter queries all
 limit=1
)

8.2 QueryIterator High Memory Usage

Cause: batch_size setting too large

Solution:

# Reduce batch_size
iterator = client.query_iterator(
 collection_name="book_collection",
 filter="is_available == True",
 batch_size=10 # Reduce from 100 to 10
)

8.3 Filter Expression Syntax Errors

Common Errors:

# ❌ Wrong: String without quotes
filter = "title == Python"

# ✅ Correct
filter = 'title == "Python"'

# ❌ Wrong: LIKE wildcard position error
filter = 'title like "Python*"'

# ✅ Correct
filter = 'title like "Python%"'

IX. Chapter Knowledge Summary

Query Method Key Parameters Applicable Scenarios
Get ids Precise lookup with known primary key
Query filter, limit Conditional filtering, moderate data volume
QueryIterator filter, batch_size Large data volume pagination query

Core Filter Expression Operators:

  • Comparison: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=
  • Range: in, like
  • Logic: and, or, not
  • Null values: is null, is not null
  • Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, %, **

item.title: Advanced Milvus Search Techniques: Range, Grouping, and Hybrid Search
item.description:
Coverage: Range search, grouping search, hybrid search (multi-vector + reranking) Official Documentation: Range Search | Grouping Search | Hybrid Search

I. Range Search

1.1 Core Concepts

Normal ANN search returns “K most similar” results, but doesn’t limit the similarity range. Range search uses radius and range_filter parameters to define a ring-shaped similarity interval, returning only results that fall within the interval.

Using query vector as center, draw two concentric circles:
range_filter (inner circle)── Exclude overly similar results (deduplication scenarios)
radius (outer circle)──────── Exclude overly dissimilar results
Only return vectors falling within the ring-shaped area

Why do we need the inner circle (range_filter)?

  • Deduplication scenarios: Exclude results almost identical to query vector (might be the query vector itself)
  • Recommendation scenarios: Exclude highly similar content users have already seen, recommend “related but non-repetitive” content
  • If inner circle not needed, set range_filter to 0 (L2/JACCARD/HAMMING) or leave unset

1.2 Parameter Setting Rules (Important, easy to confuse)

Different distance metrics have opposite parameter relationships:

metric_type Similarity Trend Parameter Relationship Meaning
COSINE Higher values more similar radius < distance <= range_filter radius is lower bound, range_filter is upper bound
IP Higher values more similar radius < distance <= range_filter Same as above
L2 Lower values more similar range_filter <= distance < radius range_filter is lower bound, radius is upper bound
JACCARD Lower values more similar range_filter <= distance < radius Same as above
HAMMING Lower values more similar range_filter <= distance < radius Same as above

Memory mnemonic:

  • COSINE/IP: Higher values similar → radius (small) < distance <= range_filter (large)
  • L2/JACCARD/HAMMING: Lower values similar → range_filter (small) <= distance < radius (large)

1.3 Code Example 

def range_search_demo():
 """
 Range search: Limit similarity interval, return only results within interval

 Parameter position: radius and range_filter go in search_params["params"]
 Note: Different metric_type parameter size relationships differ, see table above
 """
 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 # ── COSINE distance: radius < distance <= range_filter ──
 # Target: Books with similarity 0.8 ~ 0.95 (high similarity interval)
 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=3,
 search_params={
 "metric_type": "COSINE",
 "params": {
 "radius": 0.8, # Outer circle lower bound (COSINE lower similarity bound)
 "range_filter": 0.95 # Inner circle upper bound (COSINE upper similarity bound)
 }
 },
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"]
 )

 print(f"High similarity search results (similarity 0.8~0.95): Total {len(result[0])} records")
 for i, hit in enumerate(result[0]):
 print(f" {i+1}. {hit['entity']['title']}, Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

 # ── Medium similarity interval ──
 result2 = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=3,
 search_params={
 "metric_type": "COSINE",
 "params": {
 "radius": 0.5,
 "range_filter": 0.7 # Similarity 0.5~0.7
 }
 },
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"]
 )
 print(f"Medium similarity search results (similarity 0.5~0.7): Total {len(result2[0])} records")

1.4 Common Scenario Parameter Reference (COSINE)

Scenario radius range_filter Description
Deduplication detection 0.95 1.0 Find almost identical content
High similarity recommendation 0.8 0.95 Strongly related content
Medium similarity recommendation 0.5 0.8 Related but differentiated
Loose matching 0.3 0.6 Broadly related content

⚠️ Note: If parameters set too strictly (interval too narrow), may return 0 results. Recommended to test with loose intervals first, then gradually tighten.

II. Grouping Search

2.1 Core Concepts

Normal ANN search may return many results from the same “category”, causing result homogenization. Grouping search groups by specified field, returns only N most similar from each group, ensuring result diversity.

Typical scenario: RAG document retrieval

Question: A long document is split into 100 chunks stored in Milvus
Normal search top-5 might all be different segments of same document
Grouping search: Group by doc_id, return 1 from each group
→ top-5 from 5 different documents, more diverse results

2.2 Three Key Parameters

Parameter Type Description
group_by_field str Which field to group by, supports BOOL/INT/VARCHAR/ARRAY, does not support FLOAT
group_size int Maximum records per group, default 1
strict_group_size bool Whether to strictly guarantee group_size records per group, default False

limit meaning changed:

  • Normal search: limit = total entity count returned
  • Grouping search: limit = number of groups returned, total entities ≈ limit × group_size

2.3 Code Example 

def grouping_search_demo():
 """
 Grouping search: Group by field, avoid result homogenization

 book_collection grouped by is_available (BOOL):
 - True group: Most similar among available books
 - False group: Most similar among unavailable books
 """
 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 # ── Basic grouping: Return 1 from each group ──
 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=2, # Return 2 groups (available/unavailable)
 group_by_field="is_available", # Group by availability
 group_size=1, # Return 1 from each group
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available"]
 )
 # Maximum return 2×1=2 records

 print(f"Basic grouping search results: Total {len(result[0])} records")
 for hit in result[0]:
 entity = hit['entity']
 status = "Available" if entity['is_available'] else "Unavailable"
 print(f" [{status}] {entity['title']}, Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

 # ── Multi-record grouping: Return 2 from each group ──
 result2 = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=2, # 2 groups
 group_by_field="is_available",
 group_size=2, # Return 2 from each group
 strict_group_size=True, # Strictly guarantee 2 per group (may return fewer if data insufficient)
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available"]
 )
 # Maximum return 2×2=4 records

 print(f"\nMulti-record grouping search results: Total {len(result2[0])} records")
 for hit in result2[0]:
 entity = hit['entity']
 status = "Available" if entity['is_available'] else "Unavailable"
 print(f" [{status}] {entity['title']}, Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

2.4 Difference of strict_group_size 

# strict_group_size=False (default)
# Prioritize returning limit groups, each group tries to return group_size records
# With uneven data distribution, some groups may have fewer than group_size records
# Better performance

# strict_group_size=True
# Strictly guarantee group_size records per group (except groups with insufficient data)
# Performance may degrade with uneven data distribution
# Suitable for scenarios requiring strict quantity control per group

2.5 Supported Index Types

Grouping search only supports these indexes: FLAT, IVF_FLAT, IVF_SQ8, HNSW, HNSW_PQ, HNSW_PRQ, HNSW_SQ, DISKANN, SPARSE_INVERTED_INDEX

⚠️ Does not support FLOAT type field grouping, grouping by rating (FLOAT) will cause errors, need to use INT or VARCHAR fields instead.

III. Hybrid Search

3.1 Core Concepts

Hybrid search allows simultaneous search across multiple vector fields, then merges results using reranking strategies.

Why do we need hybrid search?

Limitations of single vector:

  • Dense vectors: Good at semantic understanding, weak at precise keyword matching
  • Sparse vectors: Good at keyword matching, lack semantic understanding

Hybrid search combines both advantages, typical combinations:

Dense vector (semantics) + Sparse vector (keywords) → More accurate text retrieval
Text vector + Image vector → Multimodal search
Multiple text field vectors → Comprehensive multi-dimensional information

3.2 Three-step Process 

Step 1: Create AnnSearchRequest for each vector field
Step 2: Configure reranking strategy (RRF or Weighted)
Step 3: Call hybrid_search() to execute

3.3 Code Example 

def hybrid_vector_search_demo():
 """
 Hybrid search: Simultaneously search content_vector (dense) and keyword_vector (sparse)
 Use RRF reranking strategy to merge results
 """
 from pymilvus import AnnSearchRequest, Function, FunctionType

 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 # ── Step 1: Create independent search requests for each vector field ──

 # Dense vector search request (semantic similarity)
 content_search_req = AnnSearchRequest(
 data=[query_vector], # Query vector, must be list
 anns_field="content_vector", # Target vector field
 param={"metric_type": "COSINE", "params": {"nprobe": 10}},
 limit=3 # Candidate count per sub-request
 # expr="is_available == True" # Optional: Sub-requests also support filter conditions
 )

 # Sparse vector search request (keyword matching)
 # Sparse vectors represented as dictionaries: {dimension_index: weight_value}
 keyword_query = {0: 0.8, 10: 0.6, 25: 0.4}
 keyword_search_req = AnnSearchRequest(
 data=[keyword_query],
 anns_field="keyword_vector",
 param={"metric_type": "IP", "params": {}}, # Sparse vectors use IP
 limit=3
 )

 # ── Step 2: Configure reranking strategy ──

 # RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion): No manual weight adjustment, suitable when unsure of route weights
 ranker = Function(
 name="rrf",
 input_field_names=[], # Must be empty list, cannot fill vector field names
 function_type=FunctionType.RERANK,
 params={
 "reranker": "rrf",
 "k": 100 # Control ranking decay speed, default 100, usually no need to change
 }
 )

 # ── Step 3: Execute hybrid search ──
 result = client.hybrid_search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 reqs=[content_search_req, keyword_search_req], # All sub-search requests
 ranker=ranker,
 limit=3, # Total records returned
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"]
 # filter="is_available == True" # Optional: Filter final results
 )

 print(f"Hybrid search results: Total {len(result[0])} records")
 for i, hit in enumerate(result[0]):
 entity = hit['entity']
 print(f" {i+1}. ID: {entity['book_id']}, Title: {entity['title']}, "
 f"Rating: {entity['rating']}, Combined Score: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

3.4 Two Reranking Strategy Comparison

RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion)

ranker = Function(
 name="rrf",
 input_field_names=[],
 function_type=FunctionType.RERANK,
 params={"reranker": "rrf", "k": 100}
)
# Principle: Combined score = Σ 1/(k + rank_i)
# Features: No weight adjustment needed, all search routes equal
# Application: Uncertain importance of routes, or routes equally important

Weighted (Weighted Fusion)

ranker = Function(
 name="weighted",
 input_field_names=[],
 function_type=FunctionType.RERANK,
 params={
 "reranker": "weighted",
 "weights": [0.7, 0.3] # Weights for each request in reqs list, sum to 1
 }
)
# Features: Can manually control weight of each search route
# Application: Clearly know which route is more important
# Note: weights list length must match reqs list length

3.5 Hybrid Search vs Normal Search

Comparison Item Normal Search search() Hybrid Search hybrid_search()
Vector Fields 1 field Multiple fields
Entry Function client.search() client.hybrid_search()
Sub-requests None AnnSearchRequest list
Reranking None ranker parameter (RRF / Weighted)
Filter Conditions filter parameter Sub-requests or final filter

3.6 Common Pitfalls 

# ❌ Wrong: input_field_names filled with vector field names
ranker = Function(
 input_field_names=["content_vector", "keyword_vector"], # Wrong!
 ...
)

# ✅ Correct: Must be empty list
ranker = Function(
 input_field_names=[], # Must be empty list
 ...
)

# ❌ Wrong: Each AnnSearchRequest's data passed multiple vectors
AnnSearchRequest(data=[vec1, vec2], ...) # Each sub-request only supports 1 query vector

# ✅ Correct
AnnSearchRequest(data=[vec1], ...)

IV. Summary of Three Advanced Search Comparisons

Search Type Problem Solved Key Parameters Typical Scenarios
Range Search Control similarity interval, filter overly close or distant results radius + range_filter Deduplication, recommendation diversity control
Grouping Search Avoid result homogenization, ensure diversity group_by_field + group_size RAG document retrieval, multi-category recommendation
Hybrid Search Fuse multi-route search results AnnSearchRequest + ranker Dense + sparse joint retrieval, multimodal search

item.title: Detailed Explanation of Milvus Basic Search and Filtering Queries
item.description:
Coverage: Basic ANN search (single vector, batch), filtered search (standard filtering & iterative filtering), pagination search Official Documentation: Basic ANN Search | Filtered Search

I. Core Concept: ANN Search

Milvus uses ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) approximate nearest neighbor search, not exact kNN search.

Comparison Item kNN ANN
Principle Traverse all vectors Locate candidate subset based on index files
Accuracy 100% exact Approximate, adjustable
Performance Slow, high resource consumption Fast, suitable for large-scale data
Application Scenarios Small datasets Production environment

Search Process:

  1. Quickly locate candidate subset based on index files
  2. Calculate similarity using specified distance metric (metric_type)
  3. Sort by similarity, return top-K results

Distance Metrics

metric_type Similarity Trend Value Range Application Scenarios
COSINE Higher values more similar [-1, 1] Text semantic search (most common)
IP (Inner Product) Higher values more similar [-1, 1] Normalized vectors
L2 (Euclidean Distance) Lower values more similar [0, ∞) Image features
JACCARD Lower values more similar [0, 1] Binary vectors
HAMMING Lower values more similar [0, dim] Binary vectors

⚠️ Key Point: The meaning of distance field depends on metric_type. For COSINE/IP, higher distance is better; for L2, lower distance is better.

II. Prerequisite: Collection Loading

Collections must be loaded into memory before searching, otherwise errors will occur.

def ensure_collection_loaded():
 """Ensure collection is loaded into memory, must be called before search"""
 load_state = client.get_load_state(collection_name=collection_name)

 # state is enum type (LoadState), str(LoadState.Loaded) == 'Loaded'
 if str(load_state.get('state')) != 'Loaded':
 client.load_collection(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 # load_fields optional: Load only needed fields to save memory
 # If not specified, loads all fields
 load_fields=["book_id", "title", "content_vector", "keyword_vector"]
 )

load_fields trade-offs:

  • Specify load_fields: Save memory, but output_fields can only contain loaded fields
  • Not specify: Load all fields, flexible but high memory usage
  • Vector fields (used for search) must be included in load_fields

III. Single Vector Search

Most basic search method, input one query vector, return top-K most similar results.

def single_vector_search():
 """
 Single vector search: One query, return K most similar records

 search() return value structure:
 res[0] → Results list for first query vector
 res[0][i] → i-th hit result
 res[0][i]['id'] → Hit entity's primary key
 res[0][i]['distance'] → Distance from query vector (similarity)
 res[0][i]['entity'] → Hit entity's field values (determined by output_fields)
 """
 # Generate 256-dimensional query vector (actually comes from embedding model in real scenarios)
 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector], # Single vector passes 1D list, multiple vectors pass 2D list
 anns_field="content_vector", # Specify which vector field to search on
 limit=3, # Return top 3 most similar
 search_params={"metric_type": "COSINE"},
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"] # Scalar fields to return
 )

 # result is 2D list: result[query_vector_index][hit_result_index]
 for i, hit in enumerate(result[0]):
 print(f"{i+1}. ID: {hit['entity']['book_id']}, "
 f"Title: {hit['entity']['title']}, "
 f"Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

Common misconception:

  • limit maximum value limited by collection configuration, default upper limit 16384

IV. Batch Vector Search

Input multiple query vectors in one request, Milvus processes in parallel, more efficient than multiple individual searches.

def bulk_vector_search():
 """
 Batch search: Process multiple query vectors in one request

 Application scenarios:
 - Batch recommendations (recommend to multiple users simultaneously)
 - Batch similarity calculations
 - Reduce network round trips
 """
 # Prepare 3 query vectors
 query_vectors = [
 [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]
 for _ in range(3)
 ]

 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=query_vectors, # Pass multiple vectors
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=2,
 search_params={"metric_type": "COSINE"},
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"]
 )

 # result is 2D list, result[i] corresponds to i-th query vector's results
 print(f"Total {len(result)} result groups") # → 3
 for i, hits in enumerate(result):
 print(f"\nResults for query vector {i+1}:")
 for j, hit in enumerate(hits):
 print(f" {j+1}. {hit['entity']['title']}, Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

💡 Performance tip: Total time for batch search is usually much less than sum of multiple individual searches, recommended when multiple searches needed.

V. Filtered Search

Add scalar field filter conditions on top of vector search to narrow search scope.

5.1 Two Filtering Method Comparison 

Standard Filtering:
┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────┐
│ Full Data │ → │ Scalar Filter │ → │ ANN Search │ → top-K
└─────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────┘
Filter first, then search. Good performance when filter conditions simple.
Iterative Filtering:
┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
│ Full Data │ → │ ANN Iterative │ → │ Per-item │ → top-K
└─────────────┘ └─── Search ────┘ └── Filtering ──┘
Search first, then filter item by item. Good performance when filter conditions complex.
Comparison Item Standard Filtering Iterative Filtering
Filtering Timing Before ANN search After ANN search
Application Scenarios Large remaining data after filtering Small remaining data after filtering
Enable Method Default, no extra parameters Add "hints": "iterative_filter" in search_params
Potential Issues High latency with complex filter conditions Poor performance when very few qualifying data need large iterations

5.2 Standard Filtering 

def filtered_vector_search():
 """Standard filtered search: First filter scalar fields, then ANN search in filtered results"""
 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=3,
 search_params={"metric_type": "COSINE"},
 # filter supports operators: ==, !=, >, >=, <, <=, and, or, not, like, in, array_contains, etc.
 filter="is_available == True and rating >= 4.0",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available", "rating"]
 )

 for i, hit in enumerate(result[0]):
 entity = hit['entity']
 print(f"{i+1}. {entity['title']}, "
 f"Available: {entity['is_available']}, "
 f"Rating: {entity['rating']}, "
 f"Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

Common Filter Expressions:

# Boolean fields
filter="is_available == True"

# Numeric ranges
filter="rating >= 4.5"
filter="price > 50 and price < 200"

# String fuzzy matching (% wildcard)
filter='title like "%Python%"'

# IN list
filter="book_id in [1, 2, 3]"

# Compound conditions
filter="is_available == True and rating >= 4.0 and price < 100.0"

# JSON field access
filter='details["author"] == "Zhang San"'

# ARRAY field contains
filter='array_contains(tags, "programming")'

5.3 Iterative Filtering 

def iterative_filtered_search():
 """
 Iterative filtered search: Suitable for complex filter conditions

 Principle: ANN search iteratively returns candidate results, each candidate individually filtered,
 until limit qualifying results collected.

 Advantages: Reduce scalar filtering workload (only filter ANN candidates, not full data)
 Disadvantages: If very few qualifying data, need large iterations, performance degrades
 """
 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=3,
 search_params={
 "metric_type": "COSINE",
 "hints": "iterative_filter" # ← Only difference, enable iterative filtering
 },
 # Complex filter conditions (multi-field, multi-condition) better suited for iterative filtering
 filter="is_available == True and rating >= 4.0 and price < 100.0",
 output_fields=["book_id", "title", "is_available", "rating", "price"]
 )

 for i, hit in enumerate(result[0]):
 entity = hit['entity']
 print(f"{i+1}. {entity['title']}, "
 f"Rating: {entity['rating']}, "
 f"Price: ¥{entity.get('price', 'N/A')}, "
 f"Similarity: {hit['distance']:.4f}")

5.4 How to Choose Filtering Method

The core difference between the two methods is proportion of remaining data after filtering:

  • Large remaining data after filtering (most data passes filter) → Standard filtering
  • Small remaining data after filtering (few data passes filter) → Iterative filtering

💡 Practical suggestion: When uncertain, actually measure both methods’ execution time like in filter-search-demo.py’s performance_comparison(), choose based on data distribution.

VI. Pagination Search

When needing results exceeding limit upper bound, or implementing pagination, use offset parameter.

def pagination_search():
 """
 Pagination search: Skip first N results via offset, implement pagination

 limit + offset sum cannot exceed 16384 (Milvus default limit)
 """
 query_vector = [float(random.uniform(-1, 1)) for _ in range(256)]

 page_size = 2 # 2 records per page

 for page in range(3): # Get first 3 pages
 offset = page * page_size

 result = client.search(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 data=[query_vector],
 anns_field="content_vector",
 limit=page_size,
 search_params={
 "metric_type": "COSINE",
 "offset": offset # ← offset in search_params
 },
 output_fields=["book_id", "title"]
 )

 print(f"\nPage {page+1} (offset={offset}):")
 for i, hit in enumerate(result[0]):
 print(f" {i+1}. {hit['entity']['title']}")

Pagination Notes:

Consideration Description
offset position Can be in search_params dictionary, or as top-level keyword parameter of search()
Upper limit limit + offset <= 16384, will error if exceeded
Consistency issues Results may be inconsistent across pages if data written during pagination
Performance Larger offset, worse performance (need to skip first N results)

⚠️ Large data pagination: For traversing large amounts of data (like exports), recommend using Search Iterator (search iterator), better performance, covered in later Part.

VII. output_fields Usage Tips 

# Return specified fields
output_fields=["book_id", "title", "rating"]

# Return all scalar fields (excluding vector fields)
output_fields=["*"]

# Return vector fields (large data volume, use as needed)
output_fields=["book_id", "content_vector"]

# Access JSON field sub-properties
output_fields=["details"]

VIII. Search Result Structure Quick Reference 

result = client.search(...)

# result structure
result # List[List[Hit]], outer layer corresponds to each query vector
result[0] # All hit results for first query vector
result[0][0] # First hit result (most similar)

hit = result[0][0]
hit['id'] # Entity primary key (quick access)
hit['distance'] # Distance/similarity from query vector
hit['entity'] # Dictionary containing fields specified by output_fields
hit['entity']['title'] # Access specific field values

IX. Chapter Knowledge Summary

Search Type Key Parameters Application Scenarios
Single Vector Search data=[vector], limit=K Basic similarity query
Batch Search data=[v1, v2, ...] Batch recommendations, reduce network overhead
Standard Filtering filter="condition" Simple filter conditions
Iterative Filtering search_params={"hints": "iterative_filter"} Complex filter conditions
Pagination Search search_params={"offset": N} Pagination, get more results
